content:
Effect of Non Cross Examination

MEANING
· Cross-examination is the right of the opposite party to test the truth, accuracy, and credibility of a witness’s testimony. It enables a party to challenge, explain, or contradict the facts deposed by the witness.

LEGAL EFFECT OF NON-CROSS-EXAMINATION

· UNCHALLENGED TESTIMONY MAY BE ACCEPTED
 If a witness is not cross-examined on a material point, the Court may treat that part of the testimony as admitted or unchallenged.
· IMPLIED ACCEPTANCE
 Failure to challenge a material statement during cross-examination may amount to an implied acceptance of that statement.
· LOSS OF RIGHT TO CONTRADICT
 A party who does not confront a witness on a specific fact may later be barred from disputing that fact.
· GREATER EVIDENTIARY WEIGHT
 Evidence that remains unchallenged generally carries greater evidentiary value before the Court.

IMPORTANT PRINCIPLE

· A party intending to dispute a witness’s version must ordinarily confront the witness during cross-examination; otherwise, the testimony may be treated as admitted.

LEADING CASE
· If a material portion of a witness’s statement is not challenged in cross-examination, it may be presumed that the opposite party has accepted that part of the statement.
 The Supreme Court observed that the plaintiffs failed to cross-examine the witness regarding a material fact. Consequently, that fact was treated as admitted.

2020 SCMR 214
· Sikandar Hayat and another v. Sughran Bibi and others
 [Supreme Court of Pakistan]

CLOSURE OF RIGHT TO CROSS-EXAMINATION
1996 SCMR 1967

EXCEPTIONS
· The Court is not bound to accept testimony merely because it was not cross-examined.
· If the evidence is inherently unreliable, improbable, contradictory, or contrary to law, the Court may still reject it.
· The Court retains the duty to assess the credibility and overall worth of the evidence.

CRIMINAL CASES: DIFFERENT APPROACH

PLJ 2019 SC (Cr.) 1

· Mst. Asia Bibi v. The State
 Principle: The rule applicable in civil cases cannot be applied rigidly in criminal cases.
 A criminal case is decided on the basis of the totality of evidence and surrounding circumstances, not merely because a witness was not cross-examined on a particular fact.

PRACTICAL EXAMPLE

· A witness states that the plaintiff borrowed Rs. 500,000 from the defendant on a specific date and promised to return it within three months. If the plaintiff does not challenge this statement in cross-examination on these material aspects (amount, date, or promise to return), the Court may infer that the fact is not disputed and accept it as correct.

IMPORTANT NOTE
· Non-cross-examination does not automatically make evidence conclusive. The Court may still reject unchallenged testimony if it is inherently unreliable, improbable, contradictory, or against law.
comment: Often overlooked in practice: omission of cross-exam is weighed with pleadings and inherent probability; courts still test whether a suggestion was ever put, not just silence.
